verses.commentary: Unity of language. Humanity can communicate fully. This enables both cooperation and collective rebellion.
Eastward continues the pattern of moving away from Eden (3:24, 4:16). Shinar is Babylon (10:10). Settlement replaces spreading. They stop instead of filling the earth (9:1).
Human ingenuity. Technology advances. Bricks allow massive construction. The repetition of 'let's' emphasizes collective action. They rely on their own resources.

The goal: a city, a tower, a name. Self-glorification and security. The tower reaches to the heavens, suggesting pride and perhaps idolatry (Mesopotamian ziggurats were temple towers). 'Make a name for ourselves' contrasts with God's promise to make Abraham's name great (12:2). They resist scattering, defying God's command to fill the earth.
Irony. They build up to heaven; God comes down. The tower is so insignificant that God must descend to see it. Their pride is exposed.
Unity enables anything. This is not praise but concern. Human capacity without divine guidance leads to pride and rebellion. Unlimited potential becomes dangerous when directed away from God.

God's 'let us' (plural of deliberation, as in 1:26) answers their 'let us'. God intervenes to limit collective rebellion. Confusion (balal, בָּלַל) of language is both judgment and mercy. It stops the project but also forces obedience to the original command to spread.
The very thing they feared happens. Scattering is both judgment and fulfillment of God's plan. The city and tower are abandoned. Human pride fails.

Babel (בָּבֶל) sounds like balal (confused). The name becomes a pun. Babylon, which means 'gate of god' in Akkadian, becomes the place of confusion. The nations are dispersed, setting the stage for God to call one man (Abraham) to bless all nations. Babel's scattering anticipates Pentecost's reversal (Acts 2), where many languages hear the gospel.
The genealogy narrows from all nations to one line. Shem to Abraham. This is the line of promise.
The pattern from chapter 5 resumes. Long lifespans, then death.
The genealogy continues steadily toward Abraham.
Life continues. Generations pass.
Eber, the ancestor of the Hebrews, appears.
The pattern repeats.
Peleg, the man of division (10:25), carries the line forward.
Eber has a long life. He overlaps many generations.
The line continues steadily.
Lifespans are beginning to shorten compared to chapter 5.
Generation after generation, moving toward Abraham.
The pattern holds.
Nahor is Abraham's grandfather.
The generations narrow toward the key figure.
Terah is Abraham's father. The genealogy is almost complete.
Lifespans continue to decrease.
Abraham (initially Abram) finally appears! He and his brothers are named. The narrative focus shifts to this family.
More detail about the family. Lot will play a significant role in Abraham's story.
Death before the father, unusual and tragic. Ur is in southern Mesopotamia, a center of moon worship. This is Abraham's homeland.
Marriages are noted. Sarai (later Sarah) is introduced. She will be central to the promise. Nahor marries his niece, showing the family intermarriage common in the ancient world.
The key problem. Barrenness. This creates tension with God's coming promise of descendants. How can a barren woman become the mother of nations? This sets up the need for divine intervention.
The family begins a journey toward Canaan but stops halfway. Harran is in northern Mesopotamia. Terah initiates the journey, but it's incomplete. Abraham will finish what his father started.
Terah's life ends in Harran. The old generation passes. Abraham is now free to continue the journey. The stage is set for God's call in chapter 12.
